questions.question: What is anycast and how does it differ from unicast for DNS?
options: Anycast sends to all nodes simultaneously; unicast sends to one
The same VIP is advertised from multiple nodes via BGP; the network routes each client to the nearest node, unlike unicast which has a single server
Anycast is a DNS protocol extension
questions.explanation: Anycast uses BGP to advertise the same VIP from multiple locations. The routing protocol naturally directs each client to the nearest node, providing redundancy and lower latency.

questions.question: How does anycast DNS achieve faster failover than unicast DNS?
options: When a node fails, its BGP route is withdrawn and traffic reroutes to the next-nearest node within BGP convergence time (sub-second), vs. 5+ seconds for unicast client-side timeout
questions.explanation: Anycast failover is driven by BGP route withdrawal (sub-second convergence), while unicast failover depends on client-side DNS resolver timeouts (typically 5 seconds per configured server).

questions.question: What role does the health check play in anycast DNS failover?
options: It periodically tests the local DNS service; after consecutive failures, it triggers BGP route withdrawal
questions.explanation: Each node runs a local health check (e.g., DNS query every 5 seconds). After N consecutive failures, the node withdraws its BGP route, causing traffic to reroute to healthy nodes.

questions.question: What is ECMP and how does it affect anycast DNS load distribution?
options: ECMP is a DNS record type
Equal-Cost Multi-Path: when multiple routes to the VIP have the same AS-path length, traffic is distributed across them
questions.explanation: ECMP distributes traffic across multiple paths with equal cost (same AS-path length). In anycast DNS, this means clients with equal-cost paths to two nodes have their traffic split between them.

questions.question: Why is anycast DNS more available than unicast DNS?
options: Anycast provides N-1 redundancy: any single node can fail and the service remains available via the other nodes
questions.explanation: With N anycast nodes, the failure of any one node is absorbed by the remaining N-1 nodes. Unicast has a single point of failure: if the server goes down, all clients lose DNS.
